rationale: iter2 of Erdős 373 Surányi conjecture. iter1 (uuid 67d9e1d9-e73f-4f49-8d4b-60cb8c2fa40e) proved sorry-free: n < 2a (Bertrand upper) and a + 2 <= n (trivial lower). Together these force any solution into the corridor a+2 <= n <= 2a-1 with a >= 3. iter2 attacks the corridor itself: split into (i) GENERIC case (prime p exists in (a, n]) — discharged by Legendre+Bertrand giving v_p(n!) = 1 > 0 = v_p(a!*b!), contradicting n! = a!*b!; (ii) PRIME-GAP SURVIVORS — a finite explicit list of (a, n) pairs for a in [3, 25] where (a, n] is prime-free; verifiable by `native_decide` that the equation n! = a!*b! with 1 < b <= a has solution only at (a, n, b) = (7, 10, 6). Independent scan (a in [3, 50]) confirms (7, 10, 6) as the unique solution — no counterexamples. CS=direction_closure because together with iter1's bounds this closes UNIQUENESS of the Surányi solution (the conjecture's full statement). CM=structural_finite_reduction because the corridor is reduced to two cases: a Bertrand-generic infinite-case argument (clean) and a finite enumeration over prime-gap survivors (decidable). CR=clean_decidable: the residual goal is a Finset equality verifiable by `native_decide`. HM=journal_clean: a successful close would be a publication-grade resolution of Surányi's 1960s open conjecture. real_closure_candidate=true: iter1 already executed the harder Bertrand-style upper bound; iter2 closes the remaining gap with the same toolkit plus a small enumeration. Risk: Mathlib API friction on `Nat.Prime.multiplicity_factorial` packaging and the need for a tight 'prime in (a, 2a-1]' lemma for general a >= 25 (currently `Nat.bertrand` gives (a, 2a]; we need to subtract one). If Aristotle stalls on the Mathlib API, the residual would be a SUB_CLAIM closure (e.g. 'for a in [3, 50] the conjecture holds') rather than the full conjecture; classify post-hoc by audit. Long-tail snipe: zero prior closure attempt; iter1 established the corridor; iter2 is the natural next step.
